text: The surgeon begins in the left retrorectus space, attempting to incise the posterior rectus sheath. The hernia extends along the entire midline, with no distinct hernia sac visible.
The surgeon navigates around the midline, crossing over to the right side, where the right rectus muscle is visible.
The surgeon encounters adhesions and begins to take them down, while incising the rectus sheath on the left side.
The surgeon opens the falciform ligament and brings it down, revealing a hernia in the epigastric area, suspected to be a trocar site hernia from a previous inguinal hernia repair.

The surgeon enters the right retroactive space, encountering a Swiss cheese hernia with a defect in the posterior sheath measuring approximately fifteen centimeters longitudinally by thirteen centimeters wide.

The surgeon positions the Vicryl mesh over the defect near the diaphragm and sternum, initially using a 3-0 V-lock suture. Difficulty in pulling the V-lock through the mesh is noted, prompting a switch to Stratafix sutures for easier handling.
The surgeon sutures the edges of the posterior sheath to the Vicryl mesh, adjusting the camera angle and arm positions for better visibility on the ipsilateral side.

The surgeon uses a PDS Stratafix suture to secure the Vicryl mesh, demonstrating ease in pulling the suture through the mesh. The mesh provides a good overlap over the defect without requiring a transversus abdominis release (TAR).

The surgeon performs a baseball stitch using a laparoscopic view, revealing the internal appearance of the sutured area. Some areas of the sutures are exposed, prompting the placement of figure-eight silk sutures to cover these regions.

The surgeon places a 30 cm by 25 cm soft barred mesh over the surgical area, securing it with several stitches using a baseball stitch technique. Figure eight silk sutures are used to cover exposed areas, ensuring the sutures remain superior and not facing the viscera.
